result:
At dusk, when the street lamps bloom like coins at the bottom of a fountain, I pull the grate and turn the sign to OPEN. Day people have their bakeries and newsstands; I have a florist that wakes after supper. Night-blooming things keep different hours—cereus with its tight-lipped buds, moonflower like white tongues, velvet pansies that look scandalized at their own insomnia. If you work nights long enough, the dark becomes a neighbor with a key.

Moths bump the glass as if they’ve forgotten how the door works. I keep a little lamp over the till so the bills don’t swim away. My cat, Helios, named as a joke that turned out to be an incantation, disappears into the back, where I store cut stems and the radio that only ever holds a station halfway. Outside, the street smells like old rain and warm stone. Inside, it smells like vanilla, pepper, peppermints I keep for the jittery, and tuberose dreaming of weddings that never were.

The hospice nurse comes in at ten, the hour she always mistakes for midnight. She’s still in scrubs, the color of a sky that considers a storm and declines. Her hands are clean in a way that tells you how many times they’ve been washed. “You ever have a scent that untangles your throat?” she asks, as if asking me to hand her a kind of wrench.

“Tuberose,” I say. “And mint to cut it.”

She presses the mint to her upper lip like a mustache, and I do not tell her she is beautiful because what she needs is to be ordinary for three minutes. She chooses a little bouquet of pale things, all ghosts and milk. “He kept asking if it was morning,” she says, the way people say they tried a new restaurant. Her eyes shine as if the idea has gotten sharper. I tie a bow out of raffia and slide in a card. For the night shift, I write things like good morning.

The boy with the skateboard stands outside so long I consider adding him to inventory. He finally comes in clutching a drawing of an apology so literal it aches—two stick figures, one with a broken heart drawn like a cracked egg, the other handing over glue. “Do you think this opens tonight?” he asks, pointing to a moonflower bud like a green fist.

“If you put it in water and take it to where you can keep your mouth shut for an hour, yes,” I say. “And don’t breathe on it like you’re going to blow out candles.”

He nods solemnly. “Do I say I’m sorry before it opens or after?”

“Before,” I say. “Then when it opens, you let it say the rest.”

At midnight, the man with the maps arrives. He brings a hand-drawn atlas rolled in a rubber band and crackling with carefulness. His maps are of places that aching makes: The Cul-de-sac of Almost, The River of What I Meant, The Field Where You Can Hear Next Week. He looks like someone who once slept on a train and woke up three time zones lighter. “Do you have anything that smells like the dark behind a barn?” he asks.

“Night jasmine,” I say, and he closes his eyes, nodding like I handed him a key. He buys nothing he can’t plant. He’s moving again, he says, to a town that has a bell but no tower yet. He asks me for directions to a childhood. I draw him a line that loops and never meets itself. We both know what we’re doing.

The radio says there will be meteors at two, like someone’s ordered confetti for the sky. I make tea in a chipped enamel pot and put out a second cup as if the night might be thirsty. In the back room, the queen-of-the-night waits with the arrogant patience of rare things. Its bud, tight as a fist in a pocket, points toward Helios sleeping like a question mark.

You always ask about the one I wait for. You, plural, because the city is a chorus in which everyone knows one of my lines. She came in two summers ago, hair cut short the way you do when you’ve promised yourself not to drag a net of grief around anymore. She asked if the cereus bloomed here and how would it tell her if it did. “It doesn’t tell you,” I said. “You have to watch.” She put her number on the back of my receipt and said call me when it opens, like ordering a cake.

That night, after she left, I turned the receipt over and read the other side. It said you will be lucky in strange weather. I called. She came. We stood over the plant like midwives with nothing to do. At eleven, nothing. At midnight, the air around it got sweet as if a jar of honey had been uncorked ten miles away. Then the bud let go. It didn’t open; it undid itself, seam by seam, a dress falling around a body that had never been told no. We stayed until the bloom rag-dolled at dawn. She kissed me like a witness. She said: when it blooms again. Then she moved away to be near an aunt who was becoming small. I have learned to be exact with my verbs.

So: waiting. Which is work, and like any work you get better at certain parts. I’m good at the cups of tea. I’m good at telling people where to put their sorry. I’m not good at the part where the phone does not ring and the shelf with the mugs gathers the kind of dust you only see if you look at it slantwise.

At one, Mrs. Alvarez from the bakery taps the glass, a jar in her hand, cardigan buttoned wrong. She trades quince jam for lavender that I shove into little brown paper bags. “I dreamed I forgot how to zest,” she says, grave. “I wanted to see if my hands remembered something.” I put the lavender in her hands and say, they always do. She tells me her wife came in yesterday to buy a cake and then remembers. We stand in that remembering, heavy and respectful, until the radio turns to a song neither of us likes on purpose.

At two, a girl with ink-blue nails appears. “Do you mind if I just watch?” she asks. “My grandmother used to sit with hers and I didn’t know that could be a thing, and now she’s gone and her plant went to my uncle who thinks it’s a cactus full stop.” She is all elbows and grief the size of a dining room table. I hand her tea. I point to the clockless place in the air the bud has made for itself.

We are quiet for a long time. Outside, the night leans into its own shoulder. Then the bud loosens. It is theater. It is reticence unbuckling itself. The petals reach and then change their minds and then change them back. The room fills with a smell like sugar with a history. I think of the first time something handed me a version of myself and said, tender, here.

“Is it sad?” the girl whispers, and I know she means everything.

“No,” I say, and mean only this: it opens because it was built to open. It closes because morning exists. Between those two events, there is a window big enough for two chairs.

We do not clap, but we want to. The meteors, reliable as a promise made to a child, scratch the sky. For a while, we crane our necks like pilgrims who forgot which way the shrine is. The girl cries without sound and then stops. She takes a picture and then another, although we both know cameras are for proof, not for mercy.

Helios wakes and washes his face with the practiced greed of cats. The radio sighs back into static like it’s relieved to have an excuse. The map man’s jasmine will thread a fence in a town that has a bell by spring. The nurse’s bouquet has already sat on a nightstand where a son breathed into its petals. The skateboard boy is lying on his back in a yard he never noticed was full of constellations, and the apology is opening its white mouth and saying something he would never have found on his own.

At four, I write her number down one more time and then I don’t call. I take a cutting from the cereus, dip it in rooting hormone that looks like powdered sugar, and slide it into soil. I write a note and bury it like a coin: Open anyway.

The horizon stains pale, a bruise curing. The flower fades from torch to handkerchief. I set the sign to CLOSED with the care you would use with a sleeping baby’s door. On the walk home I pass the bakery, the newsstand, the corner where the man with his violin plays only in spring. A sparrow peels a piece of morning and tests it. It holds. I keep going. The day will think I dreamed it all. I will let it. I have a shop that knows better.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
